[Mariana Victoria of Spain](/wiki/Mariana_Victoria_of_Spain "Mariana Victoria of Spain")

(Maria Francisca Isabel Josefa Antónia Gertrudes Rita Joana; 17 December 1734 – 20 March 1816), also known as **Maria the Pious** in Portugal and **Maria the Mad** in Brazil,[[1]](#cite_note-1) was [Queen of Portugal](/wiki/Queen_of_Portugal "Queen of Portugal") from 24 February 1777 until her death in 1816. Maria was the first undisputed [queen regnant](/wiki/Queen_regnant "Queen regnant") of Portugal[[a]](#cite_note-2) and the first [monarch of Brazil](/wiki/Monarch_of_Brazil "Monarch of Brazil").

*[Dona](/wiki/Dom_(title) "Dom (title)")* **Maria I** (Maria Francisca Isabel Josefa Antónia Gertrudes Rita Joana; 17 December 1734 – 20 March 1816), also known as **Maria the Pious** in Portugal and **Maria the Mad** in Brazil,[[1]](#cite_note-1) was [Queen of Portugal](/wiki/Queen_of_Portugal "Queen of Portugal") from 24 February 1777 until her death in 1816. Maria was the first undisputed [queen regnant](/wiki/Queen_regnant "Queen regnant") of Portugal[[a]](#cite_note-2) and the first [monarch of Brazil](/wiki/Monarch_of_Brazil "Monarch of Brazil").

Maria was the eldest daughter of King Dom [José I](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_I "José I") (Joseph I) of Portugal and Queen [Mariana Victoria](/wiki/Mariana_Victoria_of_Spain "Mariana Victoria of Spain"). As the heir to the throne, she held the titles of [Princess of Brazil](/wiki/Princess_of_Brazil "Princess of Brazil") and [Duchess of Braganza](/wiki/Duchess_of_Braganza "Duchess of Braganza"). She married her uncle Infante [Pedro](/wiki/Peter_III_of_Portugal "Peter III of Portugal") (Peter) in 1760. They had six children, of whom three survived infancy: [José](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9,_Prince_of_Brazil "José, Prince of Brazil"), [João](/wiki/John_VI_of_Portugal "John VI of Portugal") (John), and [Mariana Vitória](/wiki/Mariana_Vit%C3%B3ria "Mariana Vitória"). The death of King José in 1777 placed Maria, then 42 years old, on the throne. Her husband Pedro was nominally king [alongside her](/wiki/Jure_uxoris "Jure uxoris") as Dom Pedro III.

Upon ascending the throne, Maria dismissed her father's powerful chief minister, [Sebastião José de Carvalho e Melo, 1st Marquis of Pombal](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A3o_Jos%C3%A9_de_Carvalho_e_Melo,_1st_Marquis_of_Pombal "Sebastião José de Carvalho e Melo, 1st Marquis of Pombal"). The early part of Maria's reign witnessed growth in Portugal's economy. Maria had a number of national buildings constructed and renovated, leading to the completion of the [Palace of Queluz](/wiki/Palace_of_Queluz "Palace of Queluz") and the inauguration of the [Palace of Ajuda](/wiki/Palace_of_Ajuda "Palace of Ajuda") and other new monuments.[[2]](#cite_note-3) The death of her husband in 1786, followed by the deaths in 1788 of her eldest son, José, and her confessor [Inácio de São Caetano](/wiki/In%C3%A1cio_de_S%C3%A3o_Caetano "Inácio de São Caetano"), caused the queen to develop [clinical depression](/wiki/Clinical_depression "Clinical depression"). Her second son, João, then served as [prince regent](/wiki/Prince_regent "Prince regent"). With [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon")'s European conquests, Maria and her court moved to the Portuguese colony of Brazil in 1807. After Brazil was elevated to a kingdom in 1815, Maria became Queen of the [United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Portugal,_Brazil_and_the_Algarves "United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves"). Upon her death in 1816, she was succeeded by her son Dom João VI.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maria_I_of_Portugal&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Retrato_da_Infanta_D._Maria_Francisca_Isabel_Josefa.jpg/250px-Retrato_da_Infanta_D._Maria_Francisca_Isabel_Josefa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Retrato_da_Infanta_D._Maria_Francisca_Isabel_Josefa.jpg)

*D. Maria Francisca, [Princess of Beira](/wiki/Princess_of_Beira "Princess of Beira"), [Duchess of Barcelos](/wiki/Duchess_of_Barcelos "Duchess of Barcelos")*; [Pavona](/wiki/Francesco_Pavona "Francesco Pavona"); 1739.

Maria was born at the [Ribeira Palace](/wiki/Ribeira_Palace "Ribeira Palace") in [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon") on 17 December 1734 and baptized Maria Francisca Isabel Josefa Antónia Gertrudes Rita Joana. She was the eldest of four daughters born to [Dom](/wiki/Dom_(title) "Dom (title)") José (Joseph), [Prince of Brazil](/wiki/Prince_of_Brazil "Prince of Brazil") (later King Dom [José I of Portugal](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_I_of_Portugal "José I of Portugal")), and Infanta [Mariana Victoria of Spain](/wiki/Mariana_Victoria_of_Spain "Mariana Victoria of Spain"). Her father José was the eldest surviving son of the reigning King Dom [João V](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_V "João V") (John V) of Portugal. Her mother, Mariana Victoria, was the eldest daughter of King Don [Felipe V](/wiki/Felipe_V "Felipe V") (Philip V) of Spain. Maria's paternal grandfather João V appointed her [Princess of Beira](/wiki/Princess_of_Beira "Princess of Beira") on the day of her birth.

Maria's grandfather died on 31 July 1750. Her father, Prince José, then succeeded to the throne as Dom José I. As José's eldest child, Maria became his [heir presumptive](/wiki/Heir_presumptive "Heir presumptive") and was given the traditional titles of Princess of Brazil and [Duchess of Braganza](/wiki/Duchess_of_Braganza "Duchess of Braganza").

### Influence of the Marquis of Pombal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maria_I_of_Portugal&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Influence of the Marquis of Pombal")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Maria_I%2C_Vieira.jpg/250px-Maria_I%2C_Vieira.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maria_I,_Vieira.jpg)

*Maria Francisca Isabel, Princess of Brazil*. Portrait by [Vieira Lusitano](/wiki/Vieira_Lusitano "Vieira Lusitano"), 1753

King José's government was dominated completely by [Sebastião José de Carvalho e Melo, 1st Marquis of Pombal](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A3o_Jos%C3%A9_de_Carvalho_e_Melo,_1st_Marquis_of_Pombal "Sebastião José de Carvalho e Melo, 1st Marquis of Pombal"). The Marquis of Pombal secured control of the government after the [1755 Lisbon earthquake](/wiki/1755_Lisbon_earthquake "1755 Lisbon earthquake"), in which around 100,000 people lost their lives. The palace of Maria's birth was also destroyed in the disaster.[[3]](#cite_note-Pombal-4)

After the earthquake, King José was often uncomfortable at the thought of staying in enclosed spaces, and later experienced [claustrophobia](/wiki/Claustrophobia "Claustrophobia"). The king had a palace built in Ajuda, away from the city centre. This palace became known as Real Barraca de Ajuda (Royal Hut at Ajuda) because it was made of wood. The family spent much time at the large palace. (The palace was the birthplace of Maria's first child, [José](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9,_Prince_of_Brazil "José, Prince of Brazil"), English: Dom Joseph.) In 1794 the palace burned to the ground and the [Palace of Ajuda](/wiki/Palace_of_Ajuda "Palace of Ajuda") was built in its place.[[3]](#cite_note-Pombal-4)

On 6 June 1760, Maria married the king's younger brother, her uncle Pedro (later King Dom Pedro III of Portugal). Maria and Pedro had six children: José, João Francisco, João (later King Dom [João VI](/wiki/Jo%C3%A3o_VI "João VI")), [Mariana Vitória](/wiki/Mariana_Vit%C3%B3ria "Mariana Vitória"), Maria Clementina, and Maria Isabel. Only José, João, and Mariana Vitória survived to adulthood. Maria also delivered a stillborn boy in 1762.

## Reign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maria_I_of_Portugal&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Reign")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Retrato_duplo_de_D._Maria_I_e_D._Pedro_III_-_s%C3%A9culo_XVIII_%28Museu_Nacional_dos_Coches%2C_Antigo_Picadeiro_Real%29.png/250px-Retrato_duplo_de_D._Maria_I_e_D._Pedro_III_-_s%C3%A9culo_XVIII_%28Museu_Nacional_dos_Coches%2C_Antigo_Picadeiro_Real%29.png)](/wiki/File:Retrato_duplo_de_D._Maria_I_e_D._Pedro_III_-_s%C3%A9culo_XVIII_(Museu_Nacional_dos_Coches,_Antigo_Picadeiro_Real).png)

Portrait by [Miguel António do Amaral](/wiki/Miguel_Ant%C3%B3nio_do_Amaral "Miguel António do Amaral") of Queen Dona Maria I and King Dom Pedro III, c. 1777–1780

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Parte_do_Alvar%C3%A1_da_Rainha_de_Portugal%2C_D._Maria%2C_que_proibiu_f%C3%A1bricas_e_manufaturas_no_Brasil.jpg/250px-Parte_do_Alvar%C3%A1_da_Rainha_de_Portugal%2C_D._Maria%2C_que_proibiu_f%C3%A1bricas_e_manufaturas_no_Brasil.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parte_do_Alvar%C3%A1_da_Rainha_de_Portugal,_D._Maria,_que_proibiu_f%C3%A1bricas_e_manufaturas_no_Brasil.jpg)

Part of the Charter of Queen Maria, which prohibited factories and manufactures in Brazil in 1785.

King José died on 24 February 1777. His daughter, Maria, then became the first undisputed [queen regnant of Portugal](/wiki/Queen_regnant_of_Portugal "Queen regnant of Portugal"). With Maria's accession, her husband became nominal king as Dom Pedro III, but the actual regal authority was vested solely in Maria, as she was the lineal heir of the crown. Also, as Pedro's kingship was *[jure uxoris](/wiki/Jure_uxoris "Jure uxoris")* only, his reign would cease in the event of Maria's death, and the crown would pass to Maria's descendants. However, Pedro predeceased his wife in 1786. Maria is considered to have been a good ruler in the period prior to her madness. Her first act as queen was to dismiss the autocratic [minister](/wiki/Prime_minister "Prime minister"), the [Marquis of Pombal](/wiki/Marquis_of_Pombal "Marquis of Pombal"). Noteworthy events of this period include Portugal's membership in the [League of Armed Neutrality](/wiki/First_League_of_Armed_Neutrality "First League of Armed Neutrality") (July 1782) and the 1781 cession of [Delagoa Bay](/wiki/Delagoa_Bay "Delagoa Bay") from [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") to Portugal.[[4]](#cite_note-Birmingham2018-5) However, the queen suffering from [melancholia](/wiki/Melancholia "Melancholia") and declining mental health (perhaps due to [porphyria](/wiki/Porphyria "Porphyria")) made her incapable of handling state affairs after 1792.[[5]](#cite_note-google1937-6)

On 5 January 1785 the queen issued a charter imposing heavy restrictions on industrial activity in Brazil; how, for example, it prohibited the manufacture of fabrics and other products, extinguishing all textile manufactures in the colony, except the industry of coarse cloth for the use of slaves and workers; since the Portuguese colonial administration did not look favorably on the development of industrial activities in Brazil for fear of economic and, perhaps, political independence. During her reign, the trial, conviction and execution of ensign [Joaquim José da Silva Xavier](/wiki/Joaquim_Jos%C3%A9_da_Silva_Xavier "Joaquim José da Silva Xavier"), known as Tiradentes, took place in 1789.

## Mental deterioration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maria_I_of_Portugal&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Mental deterioration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/4_escudos_en_or_%C3%A0_l%27effigie_de_Marie_I_et_Pierre_III%2C_1785.jpg/250px-4_escudos_en_or_%C3%A0_l%27effigie_de_Marie_I_et_Pierre_III%2C_1785.jpg)](/wiki/File:4_escudos_en_or_%C3%A0_l%27effigie_de_Marie_I_et_Pierre_III,_1785.jpg)

[4 escudos](/wiki/Portuguese_escudo "Portuguese escudo") coin with effigy of Maria I and Pedro III, 1785

Maria's madness was first officially noticed in 1786, when she had to be carried back to her apartments in a state of delirium. Afterwards, the queen's mental state became progressively worse. On 25 May 1786, her husband died; Maria was devastated and forbade any court entertainments. According to a contemporary account, state festivities began to resemble religious ceremonies. The queen's eldest son and heir, Prince Dom José, died aged 27 from [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox") on 11 September 1788, and that same year, on November 2, her daughter Mariana Vitória died a few days after giving birth to her third child from the same illness as her brother at just 19. The queen's confessor, [Inácio de São Caetano](/wiki/In%C3%A1cio_de_S%C3%A3o_Caetano "Inácio de São Caetano"), [Titular Archbishop](/wiki/Titular_Archbishop "Titular Archbishop") of [Salonica](/wiki/Salonica "Salonica"), died almost four weeks later.[[6]](#cite_note-CUP-7) These deaths may have resulted in Queen Maria developing [major depressive disorder](/wiki/Major_depressive_disorder "Major depressive disorder").[[7]](#cite_note-:0-8) Another potential cause was her incestuous ancestry; this is substantiated by two of her sisters having had similar conditions.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-8)

In February 1792, Maria was deemed insane and was treated by [Francis Willis](/wiki/Francis_Willis_(physician) "Francis Willis (physician)"), the same physician who attended the British king [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III"). Willis wanted to take her to England, but the plan was refused by the Portuguese court. Potentially as a result of Willis' more advisory role in Maria's care, rather than the hands-on care of King George III, Willis deemed the queen incurable.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-8) Maria's second son, Dom João (John), now Prince of Brazil, took over the government in her name, even though he only took the title of Prince Regent in 1799.[[5]](#cite_note-google1937-6) When the Real Barraca de Ajuda burnt down in 1794, the court was forced to move to Queluz, where the ill queen would lie in her apartments all day. Visitors would complain of terrible screams that would echo throughout the palace.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Napoleonic Wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maria_I_of_Portugal&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Napoleonic Wars")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Maria_I_de_Portugal.jpg/250px-Maria_I_de_Portugal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maria_I_de_Portugal.jpg)

Engraving of Maria I from 1786

In 1801, Spanish Prime Minister [Manuel de Godoy](/wiki/Manuel_de_Godoy "Manuel de Godoy") sent an army to invade Portugal with backing from the French leader [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"), resulting in the [War of the Oranges](/wiki/War_of_the_Oranges "War of the Oranges"). Though the Spanish ended their invasion, the [Treaty of Badajoz](/wiki/Treaty_of_Badajoz_(1801) "Treaty of Badajoz (1801)") on 6 June 1801 forced Portugal to cede [Olivença](/wiki/Olivenza "Olivenza") and other border towns to Spain. (This cession is not recognized by the present Portuguese government, and the country officially considers those territories still to be Portuguese possessions.) On 29 September, Prince Dom João signed the [Treaty of Madrid (1801)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Madrid_(1801) "Treaty of Madrid (1801)"), ceding half of [Portuguese Guyana](/wiki/Portuguese_Guyana "Portuguese Guyana") to France, which became [French Guiana](/wiki/French_Guiana "French Guiana").[[6]](#cite_note-CUP-7)

The refusal of the Portuguese government to join the French-sponsored [Continental Blockade](/wiki/Continental_Blockade "Continental Blockade") against Britain culminated in the late 1807 Franco-Spanish invasion of Portugal led by General [Jean-Andoche Junot](/wiki/Jean-Andoche_Junot "Jean-Andoche Junot"). The ultimate Napoleonic plan for Portugal was to split it into three sections. The northern parts of Portugal, from the Douro to the Minho, would become the [Kingdom of Northern Lusitania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Northern_Lusitania "Kingdom of Northern Lusitania"), and its throne was promised to King [Louis II of Etruria](/wiki/Louis_II_of_Etruria "Louis II of Etruria"). The [Alentejo Province](/wiki/Alentejo_Province "Alentejo Province") and [Kingdom of the Algarve](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Algarve "Kingdom of the Algarve") would be merged to form the *Principality of the Algarves*, of which Manuel de Godoy would be sovereign. The remaining portion of Portugal would have been directly ruled by France.[[6]](#cite_note-CUP-7)

### Transfer to Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maria_I_of_Portugal&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Transfer to Brazil")]

Main article: [Transfer of the Portuguese court to Brazil](/wiki/Transfer_of_the_Portuguese_court_to_Brazil "Transfer of the Portuguese court to Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Prince_John_of_Portugal.jpg/250px-Prince_John_of_Portugal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prince_John_of_Portugal.jpg)

Maria's second son, Prince Regent Dom [João](/wiki/John_VI_of_Portugal "John VI of Portugal"), with a bust of his mother

At the urging of the British government, the entire [House of Braganza](/wiki/House_of_Braganza "House of Braganza") decided to flee on 29 November 1807 to establish a [government in exile](/wiki/Government_in_exile "Government in exile") in the [Portuguese Viceroyalty of Brazil](/wiki/Colonial_Brazil "Colonial Brazil"). Along with the royal family, Maria was transported aboard the [carrack](/wiki/Carrack "Carrack") *Príncipe Real*. During her move from the royal palace to the docks, she was heard screaming throughout the trip in the middle of the crowd and in the carriage. The queen's [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia") was so great that she feared that she was going to be tortured or robbed during her movement by her servants.[[4]](#cite_note-Birmingham2018-5)

In January 1808, Prince Regent João and his court arrived in [Salvador da Bahia](/wiki/Salvador_da_Bahia "Salvador da Bahia"). Under pressure by local aristocracy and the British, the prince regent signed a commercial regulation after his arrival that opened commerce between Brazil and friendly nations, which in this case represented the interests of Great Britain above all. This law broke an important [colonial pact](/wiki/Colonial_pact "Colonial pact") that had previously allowed Brazil to maintain direct commercial relations only with Portugal.[[4]](#cite_note-Birmingham2018-5)

On 1 August, British General [Arthur Wellesley](/wiki/Arthur_Wellesley,_1st_Duke_of_Wellington "Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington") (later the [Duke of Wellington](/wiki/Duke_of_Wellington_(title) "Duke of Wellington (title)")) landed a British army in Lisbon to initiate the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War"). The impact of Wellesley's initial victory over Junot at the [Battle of Vimeiro](/wiki/Battle_of_Vimeiro "Battle of Vimeiro") (21 August 1808) was wiped out by his superiors in the [Convention of Cintra](/wiki/Convention_of_Cintra "Convention of Cintra") (30 August 1808), which allowed the defeated French troops to evacuate peacefully from Portugal.[[6]](#cite_note-CUP-7)

Wellesley returned to Portugal on 22 April 1809 to recommence the campaign. Portuguese forces under British command distinguished themselves in the defence of the [Lines of Torres Vedras](/wiki/Lines_of_Torres_Vedras "Lines of Torres Vedras") (1809–1810) and in the subsequent invasion of Spain and France. In 1815, Prince João's government elevated Brazil to the status of a kingdom, and Maria was proclaimed Queen of the [United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Portugal,_Brazil_and_the_Algarves "United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves"). When Napoleon was finally defeated in 1815, Maria and her family remained in Brazil.[[6]](#cite_note-CUP-7)

Maria lived in Brazil for a total of eight years, always in a state of incapacitation. On 20 March 1816, she died at the Carmo Convent in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") at the age of 81. After her death, the prince regent was acclaimed as King Dom João VI. In 1821, Maria's body was returned to Lisbon to be interred in a mausoleum in the [Estrela Basilica](/wiki/Estrela_Basilica "Estrela Basilica") ([Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Basílica da Estrela*), which she had helped found.

Maria is a greatly admired figure in both Brazil and Portugal due to the tremendous changes and events that took place during her reign. In Portugal, she is celebrated as a strong female figure. Her legacy shines at Portugal's Queluz Palace, a baroque-roccoco masterpiece that she helped conceive. A large statue of her stands in front of the palace, and a [pousada](/wiki/Pousada_of_D._Maria "Pousada of D. Maria") near the palace is named in her honour. A large marble statue of the queen was erected at the [Portuguese National Library](/wiki/Portuguese_National_Library "Portuguese National Library") in Lisbon by the students of Joaquim Machado de Castro.

In Brazil, Maria is admired as a key figure in the eventual [independence of Brazil](/wiki/Independence_of_Brazil "Independence of Brazil"). It was during her reign, albeit through the government of her son's regency, that many of the national institutions and organizations in Brazil were created. These institutions were the precursors to their modern-day equivalents and granted large degree of power to the Brazilian colonials. While she is often called *A Louca* (the Mad) in Brazil, Brazilian and Portuguese historians hold her in high esteem.

## Marriage and issue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maria_I_of_Portugal&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Marriage and issue")]

Maria married her uncle Pedro on 6 June 1760. At the time of their marriage, Maria was 25 and Pedro was 42. Despite the age gap, the couple had a happy marriage. Upon Maria's accession in 1777, her husband became the nominal King Dom Pedro III of Portugal. They had six children:[[8]](#cite_note-9)

| Name | Birth | Death | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Joseph, Prince of Brazil](/wiki/Joseph,_Prince_of_Brazil "Joseph, Prince of Brazil") | 20 August 1761 | 11 September 1788 | *José Francisco Xavier de Paula Domingos António Agostinho Anastácio* married his maternal aunt [Infanta Benedita of Portugal](/wiki/Infanta_Benedita_of_Portugal "Infanta Benedita of Portugal") and had no issue. His death led to his younger brother becoming heir apparent and later king. |
| John Francis of Braganza | 16 September 1763 | 10 October 1763 | *João Francisco de Paula Domingos António Carlos Cipriano* was born at the [Ajuda National Palace](/wiki/Ajuda_National_Palace "Ajuda National Palace"). |
| [John VI](/wiki/John_VI_of_Portugal "John VI of Portugal") | 13 May 1767 | 10 March 1826 | *João Maria José Francisco Xavier de Paula Luís António Domingos Rafael* married [Carlota Joaquina](/wiki/Carlota_Joaquina_of_Spain "Carlota Joaquina of Spain"), eldest daughter of King Don [Carlos IV](/wiki/Charles_IV_of_Spain "Charles IV of Spain") (Charles IV) of Spain, and had issue. He was [King of Portugal](/wiki/King_of_Portugal "King of Portugal") from 1816 to 1826 as Dom João VI and titular [Emperor of Brazil](/wiki/Emperor_of_Brazil "Emperor of Brazil") from 1825 to 1826. |
| [Infanta Mariana Victoria of Portugal](/wiki/Infanta_Mariana_Victoria_of_Portugal "Infanta Mariana Victoria of Portugal") | 15 December 1768 | 2 November 1788 | *Mariana Vitória Josefa Francisca Xavier de Paula Antonieta Joana Domingas Gabriela* married [Infante Gabriel of Spain](/wiki/Infante_Gabriel_of_Spain "Infante Gabriel of Spain"), son of King Don [Carlos III](/wiki/Charles_III_of_Spain "Charles III of Spain"), and had issue. |
| Maria Clementina of Braganza | 9 June 1774 | 27 June 1776 | *Maria Clementina Francisca Xavier de Paula Ana Josefa Antónia Domingas Feliciana Joana Michaela Júlia* was born at the [Queluz National Palace](/wiki/Queluz_National_Palace "Queluz National Palace"). |
| Maria Isabella of Braganza | 12 December 1776 | 14 January 1777 | *Maria Isabel* was born at the [Queluz National Palace](/wiki/Queluz_National_Palace "Queluz National Palace"). |
